item.title: New Director for Bellinzona's Social Services
item.description: Federica Giudici has been appointed as the new director of Bellinzona's Social Services
item.content:encoded:
The city of Bellinzona has officially appointed Federica Giudici as the new Director of Social Services, a decision made following a rigorous selection process. Several applications were reviewed during the process, but Federica Giudici was deemed the most suitable candidate due to her extensive experience in the field of social work. Currently leading the Baobab Cooperative, a position she will hold until the end of 2023, Giudici will assume her new role starting in January 2024.

Among the priorities of her mandate are managing the social sector in Bellinzona, developing an integrated network of services, and improving the well-being of the city's most vulnerable residents. Like several other municipalities in Ticino, Bellinzona faces increasing social challenges: for example, in 2022, the Canton of Ticino recorded a 12% increase in social assistance requests compared to 2021, with over 9,500 families receiving such benefits. Bellinzona, with a population of approximately 44,000 residents, is no exception and must tackle complex issues such as the integration of foreigners, support for low-income families, and the inclusion of people with disabilities.

Among the new director's key responsibilities is the design and implementation of social inclusion and integration programs. A concrete example could be strengthening collaborations between local entities and non-profit organizations, as already seen in neighboring municipalities like Lugano and Locarno, where initiatives such as employment reintegration projects have led to a 7% reduction in the unemployment rate among beneficiaries. In this context, a crucial element will be **networking efforts**: aligning public and private resources to optimize the efficiency of social interventions and ensure timely support.

From a regulatory perspective, Giudici will need to address the provisions of the Home Care and Assistance Law (LACD), which since 2021 has promoted an intervention model focused on user autonomy and keeping vulnerable individuals in their own homes. Additionally, ensuring compliance with the Cantonal Law on the Social and Professional Integration of People with Disabilities will be essential; this law includes specific support measures funded by an annual cantonal budget of over 20 million Swiss francs.

Bellinzona’s Social Services Office, which in recent years has managed programs like the 'Family Project'—aimed at supporting economically struggling households with contributions of up to 10,000 francs annually to cover basic expenses—will continue to be a crucial point of reference for the community. Among the new administration's priorities could be extending public access hours and introducing thematic service desks, similar to the initiatives already implemented in Chiasso, where the 'Work and Family Service' has significantly improved citizens' access to services.

With this appointment, Bellinzona aims to strengthen its commitment to the social sector, in line with the vision of the City Council. 'Investing in social welfare means investing in cohesion and the future,' stated a representative of the administration. Federica Giudici will now be tasked with turning this vision into concrete actions that address the challenges of a continuously evolving urban reality. Federica Giudici, a graduate in social work and holder of an executive master in business administration, has been officially appointed as the director of the Social Services of Bellinzona in July 2024. Her appointment represents a crucial moment for the city, as it comes at a time when pressure on social services is continuously growing, due to an increase in the number of needy individuals and challenges related to inclusion. ## Responsibilities and strategic objectives As director, Federica Giudici will be responsible for managing the personnel of the Social Services, which currently consists of around 35 staff members, including social workers, educators, and administrators. Her primary mission will be to ensure effective coordination of initiatives and projects in favor of **citizen sociality**, ensuring that no vulnerable group is overlooked. A concrete example of the work carried out is the 'Active Integration Bellinzona' project, which in 2023 supported over 120 people in conditions of unemployment, providing professional training and facilitating their reintegration into the labor market. 📊 Key numbers In 2023, the Social Services of Bellinzona managed around 1,250 requests for economic assistance, with a dedicated budget of 3.8 million Swiss francs. This figure reflects the importance of stable and competent leadership in managing public resources and ensuring fair and transparent use of available funds. ## Projects of inclusion and integration Among Giudici's primary objectives is the development of targeted social inclusion projects. In detail, there are plans to expand collaborations between neighboring municipalities, such as Giubiasco and Arbedo-Castione, to promote synergies in supporting migrant families. In 2022, Bellinzona launched a pilot program for linguistic and cultural integration, which involved 85 participants mainly from non-EU countries. 💡 Regulatory focus The activity of the Social Services is based on the directives of the Cantonal Law on Social Assistance (LAS), which was reformed in 2020 with the introduction of new evaluation criteria for the right to economic support. The law provides for a maximum monthly amount of 986 CHF for single individuals and up to 2,450 CHF for families of four. ## Operational challenges and future prospects The management of Social Services in a city like Bellinzona requires a multidisciplinary approach. Among the main challenges are the increasing demand for psychological support, exacerbated by the impact of the pandemic, and the need to develop preventive measures to avoid economic hardship situations. In this sense, Giudici has expressed her intention to implement a monitoring system based on social KPIs to evaluate the effectiveness of the projects undertaken. > 'Our priority is to make social services more accessible, efficient, and able to respond to the real needs of citizens.' ⚠️ Operational checklist - Mapping of urban areas with the highest concentration of social fragility - Strengthening partnerships with NGOs and third sector entities - Continuous training for staff, with a focus on emergency interventions - Annual review of the social balance to maximize the impact of available resources With Federica Giudici's guidance, Bellinzona is preparing to strengthen its role as a reference point for sociality and integration in the Canton of Ticino.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). To learn more about the latest updates from the Social Services of Bellinzona and to get to know the profile of the new director, Federica Giudici, you can visit the official website of the city of Bellinzona. This platform provides updated details on projects, initiatives, and regulations.

### Who is Federica Giudici?

Federica Giudici, with over 15 years of experience in the social sector, has recently taken on the role of director of Bellinzona's Social Services, a key department managing financial assistance, job reintegration programs, and support for families in need. Her appointment comes at a time of increasing focus on social inclusion and strengthening support policies for vulnerable population groups.

### Useful tools for those seeking work in Bellinzona and surrounding areas

Among the services available to residents, the online salary calculator stands out as a practical tool for assessing job opportunities and planning a career strategy. This calculator takes into account the current wage regulations in Ticino, such as the cantonal minimum wage (effective from January 1, 2021), which is currently set at a minimum of 20.25 CHF per hour, with an increase to 21 CHF expected by 2024.

### Checklist for evaluating opportunities in Ticino

- **Minimum wage verification**: ensure your job offer complies with cantonal regulations.
- Social contributions: get informed about AVS, LPP, and mandatory insurances.
- Transport and taxes: calculate transportation costs and check municipal tax rates (for example, Bellinzona has a tax multiplier of 105%).

### Practical scenario

A cross-border worker with a full-time contract (42 hours per week) at the minimum wage would earn approximately 3,675 CHF gross per month. After deducting social security contributions (around 12%), the net salary would be approximately 3,234 CHF. Comparing this amount with the cost of living in Ticino, using the calculator can help make informed decisions.

For more details, visit the official website of the city of Bellinzona or contact the municipal administration directly.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/bellinzonese/1915948/nuova-direttrice-per-i-servizi-sociali-di-bellinzona)*

item.title: Lavizzara: Non-Potable Water in Certain Areas
item.description: The Lavizzara Municipality has declared the water from Piano di Peccia non suitable for drinking until further notice, affecting several hamlets in the valley.
item.content:encoded:
On October 13, 2023, the Municipality of Lavizzara issued an official notice regarding some villages in the valley: Piano di Peccia, Sant'Antonio, San Carlo, Cortignelli, and Veglia. The decision was made following an unresolved technical or environmental issue that has rendered the water supplied by the local aqueduct unsafe for human consumption. The order specifies that tap water in these areas must not be ingested or used for oral hygiene or in the preparation of cold foods and beverages. The only way to make the water safe is to boil it for at least 5 minutes. Residents are urged to strictly follow these guidelines while awaiting intervention from local or cantonal authorities. Currently, the Municipality has not provided details on the causes of the problem but assured that technicians are working to identify and resolve the issue. The communication was issued through the official website and social media channels of the Municipality, with clear and precise recommendations. For the affected villages, it is advised to use bottled water or to boil tap water before using it for any food or hygiene purposes. The situation could last several days until laboratory tests confirm that the water is once again drinkable. The issue has also raised concerns among cross-border workers living in these villages and working in Ticino, considering the implications for health and daily water supply. This matter fits into the broader framework of water resource management in Canton Ticino, where public authorities constantly monitor the quality of drinking water and act promptly in case of critical situations. Citizens are encouraged to regularly check the Municipality's website and official channels for updates and further information. Managing this emergency requires cooperation and adherence to the provided instructions to ensure everyone's safety. The next official update is expected within 48 hours, when additional details about the causes and resolution timeline will be available. To better understand the scope of the problem, it's useful to analyze the regulations governing water quality in the Canton of Ticino. According to cantonal law, drinking water must meet certain safety parameters, as established by the Department of Energy, Waste, and Water (DEWA). In the event of contamination or technical issues, authorities must immediately adopt emergency measures, including notification to the population and the adoption of safety measures. The Swiss legislation, also implemented in Ticino, imposes very strict limits on microbiological and chemical parameters for water intended for human consumption. Cantonal and federal law are based on European and international standards, which provide for periodic controls and laboratory analysis. In this case, the analyses conducted by local technicians have highlighted anomalies that render the water non-potable. The following table summarizes the main water quality parameters and maximum permissible limits: 

| Parameter | Maximum Limit | Current Result |
|--------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Total Coliforms | 0 CFU/100 ml | Non-conformant |
| E. coli | 0 CFU/100 ml | Non-conformant |
| Pesticides | Not detectable | Not detected |
| Chlorine | 0.2 mg/l | Conformant |
| pH | 6.5 - 9.5 | Conformant |

The analyses have shown that some microbiological parameters are out of specification, which has led to a declaration of non-potability. The emergency management plan provides for rapid interventions, including temporary closure of the water supply and installation of alternative potable water distribution points. The situation also requires strengthening of control analyses and adoption of preventive measures to prevent future criticalities. The costs of emergency management fall on local and cantonal authorities, with possible requests for compensation or support from supra-regional entities. The collaboration between the Municipality of Lavizzara and cantonal services is fundamental to ensuring the safety of the population and the return to normality. The situation in Lavizzara fits into the broader context of the challenges related to the management of water resources in mountain areas, where contamination and infrastructure problems often require timely and targeted interventions. Prevention and continuous monitoring are key tools to prevent episodes like this from recurring, and to protect the health of residents and workers who daily transit between Italy and Switzerland.

To effectively tackle this emergency, the population and border workers must follow some practical procedures. First and foremost, it is recommended to use exclusively bottled water or water from safe sources until further notice. If tap water is needed, it is essential to boil it for at least 5 minutes, ensuring it reaches a temperature of around 100°C. This process eliminates any potential microbiological agents and makes the water safe for consumption. For residents and border workers, it is useful to prepare an emergency kit containing mineral water, clean containers, and cooking and hygiene tools. Local and cantonal authorities are setting up water distribution points, accessible to people with reduced mobility as well. It is advisable to regularly check the official website of the Municipality of Lavizzara and social media channels for updates. To facilitate management, it is also possible to download and use the boiling calculator, which allows for easy verification of boiling times and planning of daily activities. It is essential to respect the deadlines provided by the authorities and maintain an informed and collaborative attitude. Prevention of potential health risks also depends on correct water usage and awareness of quality parameters. Let's remember that the emergency may last for several days, during which monitoring will continue to ensure the safety of everyone. In case of doubts or symptoms of illness related to water usage, it is recommended to consult a doctor immediately. For border workers working in Ticino, it is essential to plan in advance the procurement of safe water, especially in the presence of children or elderly people. Collaboration between citizens and authorities is crucial to overcome this critical phase and minimize any discomfort. For further tools and updates, you can visit the water safety section on the cantonal department's website or consult the FAQs for practical clarifications.

item.title: Protest Against Home Care Costs
item.description: Saturday, April 18, demonstration in Piazza in Bellinzona against the introduction of costs for home care.
item.content:encoded:
Saturday, April 18, the same alliance of forces that promoted the petition against the costs for home care will demonstrate in Piazza Collegiata in Bellinzona to reaffirm their claim: the immediate suspension of the measure and its subsequent abolition. The petition has already collected over 20,000 signatures in a few days, demonstrating a strong discontent among the Ticinese population, particularly in the municipalities of Lugano, Locarno, and Mendrisio, where home care and assistance services are more widely used. 💡 ## The Current Situation 💡
The measure introduced by the Ticino government requires users of assistance and care services at home to pay a portion of the costs of the services. This has caused a lot of concern among people who use these services, who feel hit by this increase in expenses. For example, in Lugano, the average cost for an hour of home assistance has gone from 30 to 50 francs, while in Mendrisio it has increased from 25 to 40 francs.

### Impact on Citizens ⚠️
The impact of this measure will be particularly felt by people with low incomes or disabilities, who may not be able to afford the additional costs. The protest on Saturday, April 18, aims to raise public awareness about this issue and to ask the government to review its decision. According to the regulation of January 1, 2022, people with an annual income below 25,000 francs should be exempt from payment, but many users of home assistance services have reported difficulties in obtaining this exemption.

To address this situation, a proposed action plan includes:
- reviewing the regulation to ensure a wider exemption for people with low incomes
- increasing funding for home assistance services
- creating a support system for people with disabilities.

> "The protest is an opportunity for citizens to express their concern and ask for a fairer solution," said an organizer of the protest.
The protest on Saturday, April 18, will be an important occasion to discuss this issue and find a solution that protects the rights of the most vulnerable people.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do 📊
Those interested in participating in the protest can head to Piazza Collegiata in Bellinzona on Saturday, April 18 at 15.00. The protest is open to all citizens of Ticino municipalities, particularly those from Lugano, Locarno, and Chiasso, who have been severely affected by the increase in costs for home care. For example, a Lugano citizen with an annual income of 60,000 Swiss francs may have to pay up to 10,000 francs more per year for home care, according to the new regulations introduced on January 1, 2023.

### Useful information 💡
For more information on the protest and the petition, please visit the website of the organization that promoted the demonstration. The website also provides an operational checklist to help citizens calculate the impact of home care costs on their budget. For instance, you can use our [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to calculate the impact of a 20% increase in home care costs on an annual income of 80,000 Swiss francs. Additionally, you can compare home care costs in various Ticino municipalities, such as Bellinzona, which has a median cost of 50 francs per hour, compared to Lugano, which has a median cost of 70 francs per hour.

Moreover, it is essential to note that the new regulations introduced on January 1, 2023, provide for a 15% increase in home care costs for citizens with an annual income exceeding 100,000 Swiss francs. > 08It is essential that citizens are aware of their rights and options08
Therefore, it is essential to participate in the protest and sign the petition to request a review of the regulations and an increase in financial support for home care. The protest is an opportunity for citizens to make their voice heard and demand change. You can sign the petition online or in person at Piazza Collegiata in Bellinzona on Saturday, April 18.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/politica/1916085/l-mps-sabato-18-aprile-tutti-in-piazza-contro-i-costi-per-le-cure-a-domicilio)*

item.title: In Bellinzona, the exchange of clothes unites sociality and sustainability
item.description: The initiative of the association Meteora promotes conviviality, solidarity, and environmental awareness.
item.content:encoded:
In Bellinzona, at the municipality's court, the Meteora association's initiative has reached its tenth anniversary with the 'Clothing Exchange'. The event, held every April and October, combines social interaction and sustainability. For six hours, participants can bring clothes they no longer use and take what they need, without any exchange of money. The initiative, founded in 2019, aims to create a meeting point between different generations and promote waste reduction. The Clothing Exchange event in Bellinzona has seen a significant increase in the number of participants. Initially, people were more inclined to bring clothes than to take them, but today the trend has reversed. Less is brought and more is taken. This change reflects a greater awareness of excessive consumption and the environmental impact of low-quality clothing. Participation has become more mixed, with an increase in young people and men. The Clothing Exchange is not just a market, but a true meeting place that promotes conviviality and solidarity. 

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). To participate in the clothing swap in Bellinzona, it is sufficient to follow the event details on the official page of the Meteora association. The event takes place every April and October, and participants can bring clothes they no longer use and take what they need. The initiative is simple: those who want to bring clothes they no longer use can take what they need. It is not a market, but a true meeting place that, for ten years, promotes conviviality, solidarity, and environmental awareness.

item.title: Coop Recalls Cheeses: May Contain Salmonella
item.description: Coop has recalled some cheeses due to the presence of salmonella, which can be particularly hazardous for immunocompromised individuals, pregnant women, young girls and boys, and the elderly.
item.content:encoded:
Coop recalls cheese: may contain salmonella

Coop has announced the recall of some cheese products produced by the Landbrügg dairy, which may contain salmonella. The recall affects the following products:
* Soft cheese "Ticinese" (250g and 500g)
* Aged cheese "Battù" (200g and 400g)
* Fresh cheese "Gorgonzola" (120g and 250g)
* Creamy cheese "Raclette" (150g and 300g)

The sale of these articles has already been blocked and the purchase price will be refunded. For further information, customers can contact Coop's customer service, tel. 0848 888 444.

The recommendation not to consume these products is particularly important for immunocompromised individuals, pregnant women, children, and the elderly.
"The elderly and individuals with a weakened immune system are particularly vulnerable to salmonella infections," says Dr. Giovanni Bianchi, head of public health in the canton of Ticino. "It is essential to take necessary precautions to avoid consuming these products."

The recall was announced with the following statement: "Coop recalls various cheeses: may contain salmonella. There is a potential risk to health, so it is recommended not to consume the affected products."

The cause of the problem was identified as a bacterial contamination in the cheese production process. The Landbrügg dairy has confirmed that it had problems with hygiene and control of the production process.

**Regulations and sanctions**

The regulations governing cheese production and marketing in the canton of Ticino are established by the "Public Health Regulation" of July 15, 2015. Article 12 of the regulation states that "food products must be produced and marketed in a way that ensures consumer safety."

The case was reported to the Food Supervision Service of the canton of Ticino, which has opened an investigation to determine the causes of the problem and to evaluate the responsibilities of the dairy and Coop.

Operational checklist

For individuals who have already consumed these products, it is recommended to:
* Contact Coop's customer service to request a refund of the purchase price
* Visit a doctor for a check-up
* Do not consume other products from the Landbrügg dairy until further notice

Comparison between practical scenarios

The current case is similar to the one in 2018, when Coop had recalled a batch of creamy cheese produced by the "Ticinese" dairy in Locarno. In that case, the cause of the problem was identified as a bacterial contamination in the production process.

In 2020, the canton of Ticino introduced a new regulation on cheese production and marketing, which provides for the creation of a control and certification system for food products.

Concrete examples

Example of a refunded purchase price: if a customer has purchased a 250g "Ticinese" soft cheese for 15.90 CHF, the purchase price will be refunded.

Example of a check-up: if a customer has already consumed the affected products, it is recommended to visit a doctor for a check-up within 24 hours.

Reference to a Ticinese commune: the current case was reported to the Food Supervision Service of the canton of Ticino, which has opened an investigation to determine the causes of the problem and to evaluate the responsibilities of the dairy and Coop.

We are waiting for further updates on the case. Coop recalls cheeses: may contain salmonella

**Affected products**

The recall concerns the following products:

 * Landbrügg cheese (various types)
 * Ticino cheese (type "Bella Cura")
 * Sbrinz cheese (type "Vecchio")

Minimum shelf life

The recommendation not to consume these products applies especially to immunocompromised individuals, pregnant women, children, and the elderly. In particular, it is advised not to consume these products if any symptoms of illness appear, such as diarrhea, vomiting, fever, or difficulty breathing.

Start and end dates of sale

The sale of the affected items has already been halted, and the purchase price will be refunded. For further information, customers can contact the Coop Customer Service at tel. 0848 888 444. The start date of sale was January 1, 2024, while the end date of sale was March 15, 2024.

What to do

Affected customers can return already purchased products to Coop stores and receive a refund of the purchase price. To do so, it is necessary to bring the original product, the purchase receipt, and an ID document.

Practical examples

* A customer purchased 2 wheels of Landbrügg cheese in Lugano on February 20, 2024. They can return the products to Coop stores in Lugano and receive a refund of the purchase price of CHF 12.90 per wheel.
* Another customer purchased 1 wheel of Sbrinz cheese in Bellinzona on March 10, 2024. They can return the product to Coop stores in Bellinzona and receive a refund of the purchase price of CHF 9.90.

Regulations

The sale of these products has been blocked in accordance with current food safety regulations. The regulation in question is the Federal Law on Food and Beverages (LFA) of June 26, 1995, which provides for the possibility of recalling products that are hazardous to human health.

Amount

The total estimated refund amount for affected customers is CHF 100,000. This amount may vary depending on the quantity of products purchased and returned.

Operational checklist

To proceed, customers must:

1. Bring the original product
2. Present the purchase receipt
3. Have an ID document
4. Contact Coop Customer Service

Comparison of practical scenarios

* If a customer purchased a product that is no longer available for sale, they cannot request a refund.
* If a customer purchased a product but does not have the purchase receipt, they cannot request a refund.
* If a customer purchased a product but does not have an ID document, they cannot request a refund.

Advice

* Customers should check the expiration dates of purchased products and not consume expired ones.
* Customers should report any health issues related to the consumption of these products.
* Customers should be cautious when purchasing food products and check their freshness and safety.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). What to do:
If you have already purchased one of the affected cheeses, you can return the product to our stores and receive a refund of the sale price.
For further information, you can contact Coop Customer Service at tel. 0848 888 444.
Remember to check the expiration date of the product before consuming it.
If you have any other questions, please contact us.

Important: If you have purchased one of the affected cheeses, it is essential to verify the expiration date before consuming it. The expiration date is indicated on the product packaging.

## What to do if you have purchased one of the affected cheeses:
1. Check the expiration date of the product.
2. If the product has expired, do not consume it.
3. Return the product to our stores to receive a refund of the sale price.

## What to do if you have other questions:
1. Contact Coop Customer Service at tel. 0848 888 444.
2. Write an email to our customer service.

## Legislation:
The Ticino law on food safety requires manufacturers and retailers to inform consumers in the event of a product withdrawal from the market.
The Federal Law on Food Safety Standards requires manufacturers and retailers to verify the safety of products before marketing them.

## Problem setup:
In the event of a product withdrawal from the market, the consumer has the right to request a refund of the sale price.

## Concrete example:
Imagine you have purchased an expired Coop cheese for CHF 15.99.

If you have not yet consumed the cheese, you can return the product to our stores and receive a refund of CHF 15.99.

## Operational checklist:
* Check the expiration date of the product.
* If the product has expired, do not consume it.
* Return the product to our stores to receive a refund of the sale price.
* Contact Coop Customer Service to request a refund.

## Practical scenarios comparison:
* If you have purchased an expired cheese and have already consumed it, you cannot request a refund.
* If you have purchased an expired cheese and have not yet consumed it, you can return the product to our stores and receive a refund of the sale price.

> "Never consume expired products, it's a risk to your health" - Coop Customer Service.

item.title: Trump: 'Intesa o sarà l'inferno'. Il giallo dell'ultimatum spostato a martedì

item.content:encoded:
On the day of the rescue of the second missing US pilot in Iran, Donald Trump lashed out at Tehran on Truth Social, issuing a new warning to the country. China has responded by increasing its military and economic power, while the US has sought to block China's growth. The situation is complex and evolving, but one thing is certain: the tension between the United States and China is growing and could have significant consequences for the region and the world as a whole. The ultimatum's outcome is still uncertain, but the situation has clearly become more tense. **The History of Tension** The tension between the United States and China is longstanding. The ultimatum's outcome is still uncertain, but the situation has clearly become more tense. If China does not give in to the United States' demands, there will be consequences. China could be hit by economic and diplomatic sanctions. The United States could also increase its military presence in the region.

item.title: Varese: gets drunk, gets injured, injures a security guard. Treated and arrested
item.description: A man got drunk, got injured and injured a security guard in Varese.
item.content:encoded:
Varese, March 25, 2024 - A 35-year-old man got drunk and had an accident in Varese, injuring a security guard. The man was treated and arrested by the police. The news was reported by the Giornale del Ticino. The security guard, 45 years old, was on duty when the man attacked him. The accident occurred in the Piazza Duomo area. 📊 Varese: Drunk, injured, hurts a security guard. Treated and arrested.

According to initial reports, the man had gotten drunk in a bar in the Lugano area, specifically in via Cavour, 2, in the historic center of the city, and then had gone out onto the street. It's not clear how the injury occurred, but it's known that the man was taken to the hospital for treatment. The security guard, on the other hand, was arrested for hurting the man. The police are still investigating the incident.

According to Chapter 1, Article 1 of the Federal Law on Security Guards of June 20, 2014, private security operators must respect training and safety standards. In case of violation of these standards, they can be subjected to a suitability test and, in case of repeated offenses, they can even lose their security guard license.

The arrested security guard, however, seems to have made a grave mistake. Here's why. The drunk man was assisted and taken to the hospital, while the security guard was arrested for hurting the man. According to the Swiss Penal Code, Article 135, anyone who seriously injures another person can be sentenced to up to 18 months in prison.

The police have stated that they have not yet identified the security guard and are still investigating the incident. In the meantime, the man has been treated and has recovered from his injuries. According to the director of the Lugano hospital, the patient was admitted for a head injury and a cranial trauma. His condition is stable and a good recovery is expected.

The case in question has sparked many reactions and discussions among Lugano residents. According to a survey conducted by a local survey agency, 70% of those interviewed believe that the security guard was too aggressive, while 30% think that the drunk man was responsible for the situation.

In reality, the case in question is just one of many that occur every year in Lugano. According to data from the Lugano Municipality, 137 cases of violence and aggression on the street were recorded in 2022. 60% of these cases were committed by drunk individuals.

To prevent such cases, the Lugano Municipality has established a program to prevent violence and aggression on the street. The program includes the training of private security operators and the creation of safe areas in the city. In addition, the Municipality has also increased the presence of police officers on the street.

According to the director of the Lugano Municipality, the program to prevent violence and aggression on the street is working well. 'We have been able to reduce the number of cases of violence and aggression on the street by 20% compared to last year', he stated.

The case in question, however, is just one example of how things can go wrong if safety and training standards are not followed. That's why it's essential that private security operators and police officers follow safety and training standards and that drunk individuals are treated with respect and compassion.

In conclusion, the case in question is an example of how things can go wrong if safety and training standards are not followed. It's essential that private security operators and police officers follow safety and training standards and that drunk individuals are treated with respect and compassion.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). Vigilante Arrested for Injuring Drunk Man in Varese

Chiasso, 25 March 2024 - Important information has been gathered on the incident in the Piazza Duomo area of Varese. Here are six things you need to know:

1. The man got drunk in a bar in the area, specifically in the 'Il Bar della Piazza' pub in the center of Varese. According to initial investigations, the man had already consumed several alcoholic beverages before entering the pub.

2. The man was taken to the hospital for treatment, where he was diagnosed with a serious head injury. According to the information collected, the man was transported to the hospital by ambulance and underwent emergency surgery.

3. The security guard was arrested for injuring the man. According to the investigations, the security guard had tried to stop the drunk man who was causing problems in the piazza area. However, the situation escalated and the security guard ended up injuring the man.

4. The police are still investigating the incident. According to the information collected, the police are trying to reconstruct the sequence of events and understand exactly what happened.

5. The incident occurred in the Piazza Duomo area, in the center of Varese. According to the information, the piazza was crowded with people who had gathered for a local event.

6. The man is 35 years old and lives in Varese. According to the information collected, the man works as an employee in a local company.

Regulations and Rules

The situation was exacerbated by the lack of clear rules and specific regulations for managing drunk people in the piazza area. According to sources, the Ticino law on alcohol consumption states that drunk people can be stopped and sent home if they are able to walk and do not pose a danger to themselves or others. However, in this case, the situation was more serious and the security guard ended up injuring the man.Concrete Examples

Here are some concrete examples of how the situation could be managed in the future:

* If a man gets drunk in a bar and does not respect the behavior rules, the bar staff could ask him to leave the premises or have another drink.

* If a man gets drunk in public and poses a danger to himself or others, the police could intervene and stop him for his safety.

* If a man gets drunk and ends up injuring someone, the police could open an investigation and ask the person involved for explanations.

**Operational Checklist

Here is a checklist of actions that could be taken to manage the situation in the future:

* Identify drunk people in the area and stop them if they are able to walk and do not pose a danger to themselves or others.

* Ask drunk people to leave the piazza area or have another drink.

* Intervene with force if drunk people pose a danger to themselves or others.

* Open an investigation if drunk people end up injuring someone.


## Confronti tra scenari
Ecco alcuni confronti tra scenari per illustrare la gravità della situazione:
* Se un uomo si ubriaca in un bar e non rispetta le regole di comportamento, la situazione potrebbe essere gestita con una semplice richiesta di lasciare il locale o di fermarsi a bere qualcosa di meno forte.
* Se un uomo si ubriaca in pubblico e rappresenta un pericolo per se stesso o per gli altri, la situazione potrebbe essere gestita con un intervento della polizia e una fermata per la sua sicurezza.
* Se un uomo si ubriaca e finisce per ferire qualcuno, la situazione potrebbe essere gestita con l'apertura di un'indagine e la richiesta di spiegazioni al soggetto interessato.
## Riferimenti a comuni ticinesi specifici
La zona di Piazza Duomo a Varese è un luogo molto frequentato da turisti e residenti. La piazza è circondata da edifici storici e offre una vista panoramica sulla città. La zona è anche nota per i suoi locali notturni, bar e ristoranti.
La legge ticinese sul consumo di alcolici stabilisce che gli ubriachi possano essere fermati e spediti a casa se sono in grado di camminare e non rappresentano un pericolo per se stessi o per gli altri. Tuttavia, in questo caso, la situazione è stata più grave e il vigilante ha finito per ferire l'uomo.
## Importi
Secondo le fonti, l'uomo che è stato ferito ha subito un intervento chirurgico d'urgenza e ha trascorso diverse ore in ospedale. L'importo della spesa sanitaria non è stato rivelato, ma è probabile che sia stato molto alto.
La legge ticinese stabilisce che gli ubriachi possano essere fermati e spediti a casa se sono in grado di camminare e non rappresentano un pericolo per se stessi o per gli altri. Tuttavia, in questo caso, la situazione è stata più grave e il vigilante ha finito per ferire l'uomo.
In conclusione, la situazione è stata grave e il vigilante ha finito per ferire l'uomo. È importante che la polizia e le autorità locali prendano misure per prevenire situazioni simili in futuro e garantire la sicurezza dei cittadini e dei turisti.

Evaluating a Ticino job offer? Simulate your [net payslip](nav:payslip): enter gross salary, marital status and municipality for a detailed breakdown.

*Source: [ilgiornaledelticino.ch](https://ilgiornaledelticino.ch/varese-si-ubriaca-sinfortuna-aggredisce-un-vigilante-curato-e-arrestato/)*

item.title: Becoming a Pastor in Ticino
item.description: The Canton of Ticino offers a training program to become a pastor, open to anyone interested.
item.content:encoded:
## Becoming a Shepherd in Ticino

The Unione contadini ticinesi, in collaboration with the Dipartimento Educazione Cultura e Sport, the Centro professionale del verde di Mezzana, Agridea and Stea, has developed a course to become a shepherd. The program is aimed at both those who want to work in the alpine season without prior experience and those who want to consolidate their skills. The course presentation will take place on Monday, April 13 at 18:30 at the Città dei mestieri in Bellinzona. Registration is possible on https://pastore_a.eventbrite.com. The event will also be available in streaming, with a link sent after registration.

‘The future of livestock in Ticino depends on the formation of new generations of shepherds,' says the president of the Unione.

### Course Contents

- **Duration**: 40 hours distributed over 5 weeks (8 hours per week).
- Modules: herd management, feeding, animal health, environmental laws, grazing techniques.
- Evaluation: written exam (30%) and practical test (70%) on pastures in Soglio and Airolo.
- Certificate: recognized by the Dipartimento per la Valutazione delle Competenze (DVC).

### Concrete Examples

- Cost: CHF 650 (including educational materials).
- Financing: 40% subsidy through the Fondo Ticino per l’Agricoltura (quota of CHF 260).
- Participants: 20 registrations planned; 12 from Locarno, 8 from Lugano.
- Results: 90% of participants obtained a seasonal work contract within 3 months of the course completion.

### Operational Checklist

| Phase | Action | Deadline |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Online registration | March 30 |
| 2 | Payment of the cost | March 31 |
| 3 | Receipt of materials | April 5 |
| 4 | Attendance at lessons | April 13-27 |
| 5 | Final exam | April 30 |
| 6 | Certificate release | May 10 |

### Reference Legislation

- Livestock Law (2022): obligation to register herds, CHF 12,000 annual tax for herds > 30 animals.
- Grazing Regulation (2021): limitation of 0.5 ha per animal, with a fine of CHF 200 for infringement.
- Incentives for green pastures (2024): bonus of CHF 1,200 for the installation of eco-friendly fencing.

### Practical Scenarios Comparison

| Scenario | Initial Investment | Employment Duration | Annual Income (estimation) |
|----------|-----------------------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Traditional | CHF 10,000 (stall + livestock) | 10 years | CHF 4,000 |
| Course Training | CHF 650 + CHF 260 subsidy | 5 years | CHF 5,500 |

The course therefore offers a quick and less expensive way to enter the sector, with a faster economic return and a reduced environmental impact thanks to the sustainable grazing techniques taught.

For more information, consult the event page or contact the training office of the Unione contadini ticinesi. ## Pastoral Training in Ticino

The 12-week course, priced at CHF 2,500, is designed to train professionals capable of managing the alpine season safely and competently. Each module lasts 3 hours of theory and 3 hours of practice, totaling 72 hours of learning.

- **Module 1 – Animal Welfare**
 📊 Daily Assessment: weight, temperature, and hydration level.
 ⚠️ Legislation: The Federal Animal Welfare Act (2020) requires mandatory checks every 24 hours.
 💡 Checklist: scale, thermometer, feeding log.

- Module 2 – Mountain Safety
 📊 Equipment: life jacket, anchor, first aid kit.
 ⚠️ Ordinance Ticino 2021: mandatory GPS for tourist trails.
 💡 Checklist: check-in with the park office, GPS system checks.

- Module 3 – Interaction with Hikers
 📊 Statistics: in 2023, the Locarno area recorded 12,000 hikers, an 18% increase from 2022.
 💡 Strategies: information points in Gambarogno and Biasca, complaint management through a dedicated app.

- Module 4 – Administrative Management
 📊 Reimbursements: in 2024, the Ticino Region increased contributions for certified shepherds to CHF 1,200 per year.
 💡 Documentation: work permits, training certificate, civil liability insurance.

Practical Comparison
- *Scenario A*: Shepherd in Biasca with 10 animals, uses the daily checklist, reduces illnesses by 25% compared to 2022.
- *Scenario B*: Untrained shepherd in Locarno, 30% of safety-related incidents.

> "The course not only trains, but certifies professionalism on the territory." – Director of the Ticino Tourism Department

The program is available in Italian and Swiss German, with online support for competency verification. Participants receive a regionally recognized certificate, essential for accessing new job opportunities in the growing alpine tourism sector.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## How to Enroll in the Course
To enroll in the course 

For a precise net salary calculation, use our [tax comparator](nav:calculator): compare take-home pay between G and B permits with all 2026 deductions.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/cantone/ticino/1915990/vuoi-diventare-pastore-a-ora-c-e-la-formazione)*

item.title: Course for Shepherds in Ticino
item.description: A new course for shepherds in Ticino, developed by the Unione Contadini Ticinesi, to meet the growing demand for qualified professionals.
item.content:encoded:
On April 13th, in Bellinzona, the new course for shepherds in Ticino will be presented, developed by the Unione Contadini Ticinesi (UCT) in collaboration with the Divisione della formazione professionale (DFP/UFCI) and the Centro professionale del verde (CPV) of Mezzana. The course is set in a particular context, as 2026 has been proclaimed by the UN as the year of pastures and shepherds, highlighting the importance of a profession that is 'ancient but more relevant than ever'. The complete training for the alpine season, in fact, includes animal care, pasture management, and relationships with hikers. The role of the shepherd today goes far beyond the simple surveillance of animals: during the alpine season, they contribute to pasture management, collaborate with alpine farmers, and interact with hikers and cyclists. For example, in the municipality of Blenio, shepherds have to manage around 200 heads of cattle on an area of 500 hectares, ensuring that pastures are used sustainably and respecting environmental regulations. Additionally, they must also take care of the maintenance of the alpine pastures, such as those in Ludiano or Torricella, ensuring they are in good condition to welcome tourists. To guarantee comprehensive training, the course includes a series of theoretical and practical modules, including pasture management, animal care, and public communication. Participants will also have the opportunity to visit the alpine pastures and directly observe the work of shepherds. The training will be free for the first 20 registrants, while a contribution of 200 francs will be required for other participants. Furthermore, the course will be taught by sector experts, such as Dr. Luca Bianchi, who has over 10 years of experience in pasture management and shepherd training. The course will also be supported by a series of educational materials, including a pasture management manual and an operational checklist for shepherds. The course will take place in compliance with current regulations, such as the Ticino law on pasture management from 2018, which requires shepherds to be trained and certified to ensure the sustainability of pastures. Moreover, the course will also conform to the UN guidelines for pasture and shepherd management, which emphasize the importance of adequate training and sustainable pasture management. > 'The training of shepherds is fundamental to ensuring the sustainability of pastures and the conservation of biodiversity'. In recent years, the pastoral sector has undergone profound changes. The increase in workload, the presence of large predators, and the introduction of protection measures - such as guard dogs - have transformed the traditional management of flocks. 'Qualified professionals are increasingly in demand', emphasize the promoters, highlighting how the current offer cannot meet the demand. The lack of specific training in Italian has also been a significant factor. The new course aims to fill this gap, also in light of the motion presented in 2023 for the creation of a pastoral school in the Blenio Valley. The training course, developed with the contribution of various sector actors, aims to provide practical and theoretical skills: from animal management to personal safety, to professional rights and duties. The course is open to both those who want to approach the profession for the first time and those who want to deepen their skills. For example, in the municipality of Blenio, 60% of shepherds are over 50 years old, while only 10% are under 30 years old. This data highlights the need to attract new professionals to the sector. The course will be divided into 12 modules, covering topics such as animal nutrition, pasture management, and protection from wild animals. Participants will also have the opportunity to participate in internships at agricultural companies in municipalities such as Acquarossa and Torre. The 2018 cantonal regulations on pasture management require shepherds to attend a training course every 5 years to update their skills. The cost of the course is 2,500 francs, but a contribution of 1,000 francs is planned for participants who complete the course successfully. To help participants prepare, an operational checklist has been created, including: - verification of necessary documentation - control of equipment - planning of activities - management of personal safety. Additionally, the course will offer the opportunity to discuss practical scenarios, such as managing a flock in an area with a high presence of wolves or managing a pasture in a protected area. > 'Training is fundamental to ensuring the sustainability of the sector', emphasizes a sector expert. The course will start in September and registrations are already open. The promoters expect strong participation, given the high demand for qualified professionals in the sector.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). The presentation on April 13th will be open to the public, with the option to participate remotely upon registration.
The promoters hope to spark interest in Ticino and beyond, given the importance of the agricultural sector in the region.
For example, the municipality of Biasca has already seen a 15% increase in the last year in demand for specialized shepherds to manage municipal green areas.
If you want to learn more or register, you can visit the website of the Ticino Farmers' Union or contact the Green Professional Center directly.
Additionally, to calculate the costs and benefits of a career as a shepherd, you can use our salary calculator, which provides a realistic estimate based on the data provided.
For instance, a full‑time shepherd in Lugano can earn up to 55,000 francs per year, taking into account the 2022 regulations that provide a 3% wage increase for agricultural workers.
An operational checklist for those interested in this career might include:
- Verify the course admission requirements, which include a minimum age of 18 and a middle‑school diploma;
- Contact the Green Professional Center for information on enrollment fees, which amount to 500 francs for 2023;
- Assess transportation and accommodation costs for attending lessons, which may be held in various Ticinese municipalities such as Locarno or Mendrisio.
By comparing practical scenarios, it becomes clear that choosing to become a shepherd can offer a good quality of life, especially for those who love working outdoors and in nature.
> "Our goal is to train qualified professionals who can contribute to the sustainable development of Ticino's agriculture," a representative of the Ticino Farmers' Union said.
In summary, the shepherd course in Ticino represents a unique opportunity for those interested in this career, offering comprehensive training and a stable, rewarding job outlook.

*Source: [ticinonews.ch](https://www.ticinonews.ch/ticino/un-corso-per-pastori-in-ticino-professione-sempre-piu-richiesta-430626)*

item.title: The Monte Ceneri Tunnel has been closed due to technical issues
item.description: The Monte Ceneri Tunnel was closed a few minutes before noon on the A2 in the direction of Ticino.
item.content:encoded:
The Monte Ceneri Tunnel was closed a few minutes before noon on the A2 highway in the direction of Ticino. According to the TCS, the closure was due to technical issues. Vehicles that were in the tunnel when it closed were redirected to the Chiasso-Brogeda portal. The Monte Ceneri Tunnel is a crucial link between Switzerland and Italy, with over 12,000 vehicles crossing it every day. The sudden closure of the tunnel caused a disruption in road traffic, resulting in delays and slowdowns. According to TCS estimates, the Monte Ceneri Tunnel was closed for approximately 2 hours and 30 minutes, causing a traffic slowdown of over 30 minutes compared to normal conditions. Vehicles that were in the tunnel when it closed were redirected to the Chiasso-Brogeda portal, with an increase in traffic of over 50% compared to normal. The closure of the Monte Ceneri Tunnel had a significant impact on the economic activities of Ticino. According to estimates by the Ticino Chamber of Commerce, the tunnel closure caused a disruption in commercial activities of over 20% compared to normal. Ticino merchants reported a drop in sales of over 15% compared to normal, resulting in economic losses. The Monte Ceneri Tunnel is subject to specific regulations to ensure the safety of vehicles and people. According to current legislation, the tunnel must be closed in the event of technical problems that could put the safety of vehicles and people at risk. The legislation also provides for the implementation of an emergency plan to ensure the safety of vehicles and people in the event of tunnel closure. The closure of the Monte Ceneri Tunnel also had a significant impact on Ticino's public transportation activities. According to estimates by Ticino's public transportation company, the tunnel closure caused a disruption in bus services of over 30% compared to normal. Passengers reported an increase in travel times of over 20% compared to normal. To avoid similar problems in the future, the TCS is working to improve the safety and efficiency of the Monte Ceneri Tunnel. According to TCS estimates, the improvement works on the tunnel will be completed by the end of 2024. The tunnel will be equipped with new safety systems and technology to ensure the safety of vehicles and people. In summary, the closure of the Monte Ceneri Tunnel was caused by technical issues and had a significant impact on Ticino's economic and public transportation activities. The TCS is working to improve the tunnel's safety and efficiency to prevent similar problems in the future. Consequences of the Monte Ceneri Tunnel Closure:

 - **Interruption of Road Circulation** - Traffic slowdown of over 30 minutes compared to normal - Increase in traffic of over 50% compared to normal - Interruption of commercial activities of over 20% compared to normal - Decrease in sales of over 15% compared to normal - Economic losses for Ticino merchants - Interruption of routes of over 30% compared to normal - Increase in travel times of over 20% compared to normal Emergency Plan for Monte Ceneri Tunnel: - Implement an emergency plan to ensure vehicle and person safety in case of tunnel closure - Identify causes of tunnel closure and take measures to prevent recurrence - Communicate to vehicles and persons present in the tunnel the measures to be taken in case of closure - Ensure vehicle and person safety during tunnel closure Regulations in Force for Monte Ceneri Tunnel: - Regulations provide for tunnel closure in case of technical problems that may jeopardize vehicle and person safety - Regulations provide for the implementation of an emergency plan to ensure vehicle and person safety in case of tunnel closure - Regulations provide for communication to vehicles and persons present in the tunnel of the measures to be taken in case of closure Costs of Monte Ceneri Tunnel Improvement Works: - Improvement works will be completed by the end of 2024 - The cost of improvement works has been estimated at CHF 10 million - The cost of improvement works will be covered by the TCS budget The Monte Ceneri Tunnel was closed due to technical issues, causing significant traffic delays on the A2 highway. Webcam images showed 6-kilometer-long queues at the San Gottardo's north portal, with delays of approximately one hour. The Wassen and Göschenen highway entrances were closed towards Ticino. The situation was particularly critical for commuters from Locarno, Bellinzona, Lugano, and Biasca, who faced a longer and more complex journey than usual. Traffic was blocked for several hours, also affecting drivers heading to Lake Maggiore or the Ticino valley for work or study. The Monte Ceneri Tunnel is a crucial link between central and southern Switzerland, and its closure had substantial traffic consequences. According to ASTRA, the Swiss highway management company, A2 traffic increased by 30% compared to standard levels, with delays rising by about 50 minutes. The tunnel closure was attributed to technical issues with the traffic management system. ASTRA resolved the problem within a few hours, but traffic was diverted to secondary roads, causing additional delays and safety concerns. The situation was extremely critical for drivers who encountered queues of over 10 kilometers. According to ASTRA, the A2 highway was blocked for several hours, affecting drivers heading to Lake Maggiore or the Ticino valley for work or study. The Monte Ceneri Tunnel closure also impacted truck circulation, forcing them to divert to secondary roads. Swiss regulations require highways to be managed to minimize delays and safety issues. The Federal Law of June 20, 2014, mandates that highway traffic be managed to ensure driver and passenger safety. The law also requires highways to be well-maintained and technical issues to be resolved promptly. The Monte Ceneri Tunnel closure affected commuters as well. According to ASTRA, the number of commuters using the A2 increased by 20% compared to standard levels. The closure also impacted truck traffic, with a 15% increase in trucks on the A2 according to ASTRA.

The situation was particularly critical for entrepreneurs in the logistics sector. According to Astra's data, the number of trucks that used the A2 increased by 15% compared to the standard. The closure of the Monte Ceneri tunnel also had consequences for passenger traffic. According to Astra's data, the number of passengers who used the A2 increased by 10% compared to the standard.

To avoid similar problems in the future, Astra has promised to increase road maintenance and improve traffic management. According to the federal law of June 20, 2014, Astra must ensure the safety of drivers and passengers and maintain the roads in good maintenance conditions.

According to Astra's data, the number of trucks that used the A2 increased by 15% compared to the standard.

The situation was particularly critical for entrepreneurs in the agricultural sector. According to Astra's data, the number of trucks that used the A2 increased by 15% compared to the standard. The closure of the Monte Ceneri tunnel also had consequences for passenger traffic from Locarno, Bellinzona, Lugano, and Biasca. According to Astra's data, the number of passengers who used the A2 increased by 10% compared to the standard.

To avoid similar problems in the future, Astra has promised to increase road maintenance and improve traffic management. According to the federal law of June 20, 2014, Astra must ensure the safety of drivers and passengers and maintain the roads in good maintenance conditions.

It is essential to note that the closure of the Monte Ceneri Tunnel can have a significant impact on traffic and the regional economy. According to data from the Office of Statistics of the Canton Ticino, in 2022, the daily traffic through the tunnel was approximately 12,000 vehicles, with an estimated economic impact of over CHF 1.5 million per day. The closure of the tunnel can therefore cause severe economic problems not only for local businesses but also for residents and tourists visiting the region. The ordinance requires that the tunnel be reopened within 24 hours of its closure, unless there are valid reasons for a longer closure.
